ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for the combined chicken-and-fries dish; closest parent 'fried chicken food product' (FOODON_00002490) used as anchor. French fries component not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers fried chicken meat; no combined chicken-and-fries composite code exists in FoodEx2. Used as closest parent for the chicken component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept identified for this combined fried chicken and fries dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g, comprising roughly 150 g of fried chicken pieces and 150 g of French fries, as typically plated at a Japanese curry restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter (NDB 05062) combined with French fries, frozen, oven-heated (NDB 11353) in approximate 50:50 ratio
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS entry for a combined chicken-and-fries dish served at a Japanese curry restaurant. Values estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for batter-fried chicken (NDB 05062, ~50% weight) and oven-heated frozen French fries (NDB 11353, ~50% weight), then computing a weighted per-100g composite. This is a reasonable approximation for a restaurant-style serving. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — French fries / fried potato GI reference; combined dish estimated using carbohydrate-weighted average of fried chicken (GI ~0 for protein-fat dominant component) and French fries (GI ~54–63 range; conservative estimate 54 used)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a combined chicken-and-fries dish. The GI of the dish is dominated by the French fries component. French fries have a reported GI of approximately 54–63 in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021). The fried chicken component contributes negligible glycemic load due to its low carbohydrate content. A conservative GI of 54 is assigned to the composite dish, reflecting the fries-dominant carbohydrate contribution. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: (54 × 22.0 × 3) / 100 = 35.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~1140 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or avoid this dish, as a single 300 g serving provides approximately 1140 mg sodium, representing nearly 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~54) and high glycemic load per serving (~35.6 g) driven by French fries carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with this dish; while the GI is at the low-medium boundary, the large carbohydrate load from the fries component (~66 g per 300 g serving) can cause significant postprandial glucose elevation, and the high fat content may impair insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat, trans fat, and high sodium from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, this dish presents moderate concern due to its saturated fat (~3.2 g/100 g), trace trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g from frying oil), and high sodium content; occasional consumption in small portions is preferable to regular intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken and Fries is a popular combination dish consisting of deep-fried chicken pieces served alongside French fries (fried potato strips). In the context of field survey data, this dish is offered at Maji Curry (Funan Mall), a Japanese curry restaurant, suggesting the chicken may be prepared in a Japanese style such as karaage (Japanese-style fried chicken marinated in soy sauce, mirin, and ginger) or katsu (breaded and deep-fried chicken cutlet), accompanied by Western-style French fries. The dish delivers a satisfying contrast of crispy, golden-brown exterior with juicy chicken meat and soft, starchy fries. Nutritionally, it is a calorie-dense meal (~265 kcal per 100 g; ~795 kcal per 300 g serving) with moderate protein (~14.5 g/100 g), significant fat (~13.8 g/100 g) from deep-frying, and a substantial carbohydrate load (~22 g/100 g) primarily from the potato component. Sodium content is high (~380 mg/100 g), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 54 (low-medium boundary), but the glycemic load per serving is high (~35.6 g), which is relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish contains gluten (from wheat-based batter or breading) and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. It is generally considered halal when prepared with halal-certified chicken and oil.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken and fries?
Japanese fried chicken with fries nutrition
Is chicken and fries high in sodium?
Can diabetics eat chicken and fries?

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for this specific combined dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for batter-fried chicken and oven-heated French fries in a 50:50 weight ratio. The GI is an analogous estimate based on the French fries component from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from a 50:50 weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy batter-fried chicken (NDB 05062) and oven-heated frozen French fries (NDB 11353). Actual restaurant values may differ depending on preparation method (karaage vs. katsu vs. plain fried), oil type, and portion ratio. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimate. 3. GI of 54 is a conservative analogous estimate based on French fries GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021, Diabetes Care); the fried chicken component contributes negligible glycemic carbohydrate. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 4. Halal status is assumed based on typical Japanese curry restaurant practice in Singapore; not independently verified. 5. The food_name has been normalized from 'Chicken & Fries.' (menu-style with punctuation) to the canonical English form 'Chicken and Fries'. 6. Cuisine is retained as Japanese per the source restaurant context (Maji Curry, a Japanese curry restaurant).

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full record based on input and approved sources. Identified that vitamin_d_mcg sentinel value (-1) required explicit documentation in notes. Verified GI category: GI=54 ≤ 55 → 'low' confirmed. Verified all aliases_by_language strings present in flat aliases list. Verified ontology match_type values are within allowed set (exact/closest_parent/none). Confirmed zero-macro rule not triggered (no core macro is zero).

Iteration 2: Addressed validator feedback regarding g3_numeric_hygiene. The note for vitamin_d_mcg was rephrased to be more direct: 'vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimate.' Removed the potentially misleading note 7 from the previous draft, as all listed nutrients actually had numeric values and were not -1. All hard gates and Q1–Q8 now pass.
